era_context: The Hellenistic world in the immediate aftermath of Alexander's death (323 BCE). The Diadochi (Alexander's successors) are competing for control of his fragmented empire. The Battle of Ipsus (301 BCE) is a pivotal moment in this struggle, determining the shape of the Hellenistic kingdoms for the next century. Simultaneously, the Mauryan Empire is consolidating in India, and China remains in the Warring States period.

events.title: Battle of Ipsus: Seleucus and Lysimachus defeat Antigonus
events.description: The Battle of Ipsus is fought between the Diadochi (successors of Alexander the Great) competing for control of his empire. Seleucus I and Lysimachus, allied against Antigonus I Monophthalmus and his son Demetrius, defeat their rivals decisively. Antigonus is killed in the battle. The victory reshapes the Hellenistic world: Seleucus gains control of Syria, Mesopotamia, and the eastern territories; Lysimachus secures Anatolia and Thrace; Ptolemy retains Egypt; and Demetrius retains Macedonia and Greece. This battle effectively ends the possibility of reunifying Alexander's empire and establishes the three major Hellenistic kingdoms that will dominate the Mediterranean and Near East for the next two centuries.
events.certainty_note: The Battle of Ipsus is one of the best-attested events of the Hellenistic period, described in multiple Greek historical sources. The date (301 BCE) is secure, and the broad outline of the battle and its consequences are well-established.
cross_references: Related to -323-001 (Alexander's death and division of empire)

events.title: Chandragupta Maurya's reign continues; Mauryan expansion
events.description: Chandragupta Maurya continues his consolidation of the Indian subcontinent. By 301 BCE, the Mauryan Empire has expanded significantly, incorporating much of northern and central India. The empire's administrative structure, military organization, and trade networks are becoming increasingly sophisticated. Chandragupta's reign represents the first major attempt to create a unified Indian state.

sources.name: Megasthenes, Indica (Greek ambassador's account, preserved in later sources)

events.certainty_note: Chandragupta's reign is dated c. 322–298 BCE. The year 301 BCE falls within this period, but no specific events are documented for this exact year. The entry reflects the broader context of Mauryan expansion during this period.

events.title: Demetrius I Poliorcetes retains control of Macedonia and Greece
events.description: Following the Battle of Ipsus, Demetrius I Poliorcetes (the Besieger), son of the defeated Antigonus, retains control of Macedonia and much of Greece. Though his father is killed at Ipsus, Demetrius's military reputation and control of key strongholds allow him to maintain his position. He becomes one of the major Hellenistic rulers and will continue to play a significant role in Mediterranean politics for decades.
events.certainty_note: Demetrius's retention of Macedonia and Greece after Ipsus is well-attested in Greek historical sources.

events.title: Ptolemy I consolidates Ptolemaic Egypt
events.description: Ptolemy I Soter, one of Alexander's generals, continues to consolidate his control over Egypt and the eastern Mediterranean. By 301 BCE, Ptolemy has secured Egypt as his domain and is expanding Ptolemaic influence in the Aegean and Levantine regions. He establishes Alexandria as a major cultural and administrative center, attracting scholars and artists from across the Greek world.
key_figures: Ptolemy I Soter (founder of Ptolemaic dynasty)
events.certainty_note: Ptolemy's consolidation of Egypt is well-attested, though specific events for 301 BCE are not documented. The entry reflects the broader context of Ptolemaic expansion during this period.

events.title: Qin state continues military expansion in China
events.description: The Qin state, under King Zheng (later Qin Shi Huang), continues its military campaigns to unify China. By 301 BCE, Qin is the dominant military power in the Warring States period, employing advanced tactics and legalist administrative principles. The state is systematically conquering neighboring states and consolidating control over northern and central China.
key_figures: King Zheng of Qin (later Qin Shi Huang)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: Qin's military dominance during this period is attested in the Shiji, though specific campaigns for 301 BCE are not clearly documented. The entry reflects the broader context of Qin expansion during the late Warring States period.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Ipsus is sometimes portrayed as a decisive end to the Diadochi wars, but in fact, conflicts continued for decades afterward. Demetrius I remained a major power and continued to challenge the settlement established at Ipsus. The notion that Ipsus 'settled' the Hellenistic world is an oversimplification. Additionally, the exact location of the Battle of Ipsus has been debated by scholars; while Phrygia is the traditional location, some have questioned this identification.
historiographic_note: The year 301 BCE is well-documented for Hellenistic history due to the significance of the Battle of Ipsus, which is described in multiple Greek historical sources (though written centuries later). However, these sources are not contemporary; they were compiled by Plutarch, Diodorus, and Appian in the Roman period. Indian and Chinese history for this year are less well-documented in contemporary sources. The surviving record is heavily weighted toward Mediterranean and Near Eastern events; the historical significance of events in India and China may be underrepresented in the available sources.

graph_edges.note: Battle of Ipsus leads to Seleucus's consolidation of the eastern territories
Demetrius retains Macedonia and Greece despite his father's defeat at Ipsus
Ptolemy consolidates Egypt while Ipsus reshapes the Hellenistic world
